--- Page 1 ---
STL-SVPIO: Signal Temporal Logic guided Stein Variational
Path Integral Optimization
Hongrui Zheng1, Zirui Zang1, Ahmad Amine1, Cristian Ioan Vasile2, Rahul Mangharam1
AbstractвҖ” Signal Temporal Logic (STL) enables formal spec-
ification of complex spatiotemporal constraints for robotic
task planning. However, synthesizing long-horizon continuous
control trajectories from complex STL specifications is funda-
mentally challenging due to the nested structure of STL robust-
ness objectives. Existing solver-based methods, such as Mixed-
Integer Linear Programming (MILP), suffer from exponential
scaling, whereas sampling methods, such as Model-Predictive
Path Integral control (MPPI), struggle with sparse, long-
horizon costs. We introduce Signal Temporal Logic guided Stein
Variational Path Integral Optimization (STL-SVPIO), which
reframes STL as a globally informative, differentiable reward-
shaping mechanism. By leveraging Stein Variational Gradient
Descent and differentiable physics engines, STL-SVPIO trans-
ports a mutually repulsive swarm of control particles toward
high robustness regions. Our method transforms sparse logical
satisfaction into tractable variational inference, mitigating the
severe local minima traps of standard gradient-based methods.
We demonstrate that STL-SVPIO significantly outperforms ex-
isting methods in both robustness and efficiency for traditional
STL tasks. Moreover, it solves complex long-horizon tasks,
including multi-agent coordination with synchronization and
queuing while baselines either fail to discover feasible solutions,
or become computationally intractable. Finally, we use STL-
SVPIO in agile robotic motion planning tasks with nonlinear
dynamics, such as 7-DoF manipulation and half cheetah back
flips to show the generalizability of our algorithm.
I. INTRODUCTION
Modern robotic and autonomous systems, including multi-
agent teams, high-dimensional manipulators, and humanoids,
are increasingly required to execute temporally extended and
complex behaviors. In reinforcement learning, these objec-
tives are typically encoded through meticulously designed
reward functions [1]. However, for tasks with rich tempo-
ral structures, rewards are often challenging to engineer,
resulting in sparse and delayed feedback that exacerbates
temporal credit assignment and yields brittle solutions [1],
[2]. Although recent advances in reward design via LLMs [3]
show impressive results in dexterous manipulation and lo-
comotion tasks, their applicability to long-horizon planning
problems with explicit temporal-logic constraints is currently
unclear. On the other hand, Signal Temporal Logic (STL)
provides a mathematically rigorous and expressive specifica-
tion language for complex spatiotemporal task requirements
in continuous systems [4], [5]. In contrast to reward-based
formulations, positive STL robustness directly certifies task
1Department of Electrical and Systems Engineering, University of Penn-
sylvania, Philadelphia, PA, USA. 2Mechanical Engineering and Mechan-
ics Department, Lehigh University, Bethlehem, PA, USA. Correspond to
hongruiz@seas.upenn.edu.
satisfaction. Despite its expressiveness, synthesizing contin-
uous high-dimensional control trajectories that satisfy STL
specifications remains a notoriously difficult open problem.
As the planning horizon increases, the feasible solution
set induced by conjunction-heavy, deadline-constrained STL
often shrinks drastically, resulting in severe optimization
difficulties [5], [6]. Even when the problem is feasible, the
size of the optimization problem in traditional STL solvers
(e.g. MILP) grows exponentially with specification complex-
ity in the worst case, often rendering them computationally
intractable for realistic robotic systems [6], [7].
The overarching goal of this paper is to enable tractable,
continuous control for highly complex spatiotemporal tasks
by synergizing SVGD and STL: leveraging STL for expres-
sive, differentiable reward shaping, while utilizing SVGD
to navigate the multi-modal optimization landscapes. Our
contributions in this paper are threefold:
1) We introduce STL-SVPIO, a variational inference
framework for long-horizon task and motion planning.
We provide a principled derivation of the resulting
inference formulation.
2) We show that STL specifications can be effectively
used as a differentiable shaping signal for motion
planning, extending their applicability to agile robotic
behaviors without relying on handcrafted rewards.
3) We analyze how particle-based variational inference
mitigates the non-convexity and multi-modality in cost
induced by STL constraints, and demonstrate our
method in task satisfaction.
We showcase STL-SVPIO across a diverse set of tasks,
including long-horizon navigation, multi-agent coordination
with synchronization and ordering constraints, agile loco-
motion, and torque-controlled manipulation. The paper is
organized as follows: in Section III, we introduce our
variational inference problem; in Section IV, we introduce
our proposed framework and how it modifies the original
inference problem; and in Section V, we present simulation
results on various benchmarks.
II. RELATED WORK
STL provides a quantitative robustness evaluation that
indicates how strongly the specification is satisfied or vi-
olated [4], [5]. Robot task and motion planning under STL
specifications remains challenging. The existing literature on
control synthesis under STL specifications can be broadly
organized into three categories:
Mixed Integer Linear Programming (MILP) yields exact
solutions to STL specifications under chosen discretiza-
arXiv:2603.13333v1  [cs.RO]  6 Mar 2026


--- Page 2 ---
tion and relaxations [8], [6], and has been widely applied
to Model Predictive Control (MPC) [9] and multi-agent
motion planning [10], [11]. Although MILP solvers pro-
vide clean solutions to simple problems, they scale poorly
with specification complexity. While improved encodings [7]
and partial-satisfaction frameworks [12] mitigate this issue,
MILP becomes intractable for highly non-linear systems and
complicated collaborative tasks [8].
Gradient-based Optimization methods have been proposed
to alleviate the computational burden associated with MILP-
based solvers. Smooth approximations are proposed to enable
gradient descent for control synthesis [13], [14]. This concept
was extended by STLCG [15], [16], which parses STL for-
mulae into computation graphs and obtains the gradients via
backpropagation. This facilitated the use of neural networks
in STL planning [17]. However, they are susceptible to being
trapped in local optima due to the non-convex landscape of
STL robustness, severely limiting their practicality.
Sampling-based Methods provide a probabilistic alterna-
tive for handling the non-convexity of STL specifications.
Rapidly-Exploring Random Trees (RRT) are used to search
for feasible trajectories under STL specifications [18], [19],
[20]. These approaches either use STL fragments or impose
strict constraints on the admissible sampling domain. Al-
though effective in simple task requirements, these meth-
ods are not directly comparable to our proposed approach.
Building on path integral control theory [21], [22], determin-
istic path integral optimization is used to directly minimize
STL costs [23]. However, these sampling-based methods are
fundamentally bottlenecked by the curse of dimensionality.
As the planning horizon and task complexity increase, the
probability of randomly sampling a valid sequence decays
exponentially, making convergence increasingly unlikely in
practice.
Our work leverages Stein Variational Gradient Descent
(SVGD) [24]. Unlike standard gradient descent, which can
collapse to a single mode, SVGD maintains a diverse set
of particles that approximate the target distribution. This
method has been utilized in Stein Variational Model Pre-
dictive Control (SVMPC) [25], which offers improved ex-
ploration capabilities compared to Model Predictive Path
Integral (MPPI) control [22]. To the best of our knowledge,
STL-SVPIO is the first framework to explicitly bridge dif-
ferentiable STL with Stein Variational inference, using the
logicвҖҷs gradient as a global guiding potential to solve the
long-horizon sparse problem that poses significant challenges
for existing approaches.
III. PROBLEM FORMULATION
A. Preliminary: Signal Temporal Logic (STL)
Syntax and Grammar: STL expresses spatiotemporal
requirements over real-valued trajectories s [26]. In this
work, we use STL formulae to describe the tasks assigned
to agents. The syntax of STL is defined as
П• := вҠӨ| Вөg | В¬П• | П•1 вҲ§П•2 | П•1 U[t1,t2] П•2,
(1)
where вҠӨis the truth value, Вөg := g(s(t)) вүҘ0 is an atomic
predicate whose truth value is determined by the value of g :
Rn вҶ’R. Symbols вҲ§and В¬ are Boolean operators вҖңconjunc-
tionвҖқ and вҖңnegationвҖқ respectively. U is the temporal operator
вҖңuntilвҖқ, which denotes that formula П•1 has to hold until П•2
holds in the time interval [t1, t2]. Other common operators
can be defined in terms of these primitives. Disjunction can
be defined as П•1 вҲЁП•2 := В¬(В¬П•1 вҲ§В¬П•2). вҖңEventuallyвҖқ can be
defined as вҷў[t1,t2] П• := вҠӨU[t1,t2] П•. вҖңAlwaysвҖқ can be defined
as в–Ў[t1,t2] П• := В¬ вҷў[t1,t2] В¬П•. вҖңImplicationвҖқ can be defined
as П•1 =вҮ’П•2 := В¬П•1 вҲЁП•2.
STL Quantitative Semantics: To measure how strongly
a signal satisfies or violates П•, STL admits a quantitative
semantics called robustness ПҒП•(s, t) вҲҲR such that ПҒ > 0
implies satisfaction and ПҒ < 0 implies violation [5], [4]. For
the syntax in Equation (1), a standard robustness on a signal
s is defined as follows:
ПҒвҠӨ= вҲһ,
ПҒВөg(s, t) = g(s(t)),
ПҒВ¬П•(s, t) = вҲ’ПҒП•(s, t),
ПҒП•1вҲ§П•2(s, t) = min
␀ПҒП•1(s, t), ПҒП•2(s, t)

,
ПҒП•1 U[a,b] П•2(s, t) =
max
tвҖІвҲҲ[t+a,t+b] min

ПҒП•2(s, tвҖІ),
min
tвҖІвҖІвҲҲ[t,tвҖІ] ПҒП•1(s, tвҖІвҖІ)

.
(2)
Differentiable STL: Recent work [13], [15], [16] in-
troduced smooth max and min approximators (e.g., Log-
SumExp) and subsequently automatic parsing of STL into
computational graphs. This enables backpropagation through
the robustness value of a trajectory to the states in the
trajectory. With differentiable dynamics, this enables end-
to-end gradient computation.
B. Optimization Problem
Consider a time-invariant discrete-time dynamical sys-
tem xt+1 = f(xt, ut) where x вҲҲX is the state and
u вҲҲU is the control input. Bold variables denote vectors.
We obtain the state trajectory by integrating the dynamics:
x0:H = {x0, f(x0, u0), f(f(x0, u0), u1), . . .}. We define
our optimization objective as the robustness value ПҒП•(x0:H),
which quantifies how well a trajectory satisfies П•. For a
given finite-horizon H, we seek a control sequence u =
{u0, . . . , uHвҲ’1} that satisfies a task specification П• defined
in STL by maximizing the robustness score:
uвҲ—
0:HвҲ’1 = arg max
uвҲҲU
(ПҒП•(x0:H)),
s.t.
xt+1 = f(xt, ut)
вҲҖt вҲҲ[0 . . . H вҲ’1].
(3)
IV. METHODOLOGY
In this section, we formulate path integral control as a vari-
ational inference problem to optimize STL robustness. Be-
cause complex STL specifications create highly non-convex,
multi-modal cost landscapes, we propose a non-parametric
approach using Stein Variational Gradient Descent (SVGD).
Our resulting algorithm, STL-SVPIO, iteratively transports a


--- Page 3 ---
Fig. 1.
Overview of the STL-SVPIO Optimization Algorithm.
population of control particles toward an optimal distribution
by balancing an attractive force driven by STL robustness
gradients with a repulsive force that promotes diversity and
prevents mode collapse. We show a flowchart overview of
our proposed algorithm in Figure 1.
A. Path Integral Control as Variational Inference
Let P denote a prior distribution over control sequences
with density p(u). Following the maximum entropy princi-
ple [27], the resulting optimal control distribution takes the
Gibbs form:
PвҲ—: pвҲ—(u) = 1
Z exp

вҲ’1
О»J(u)

p(u),
(4)
where J(u) is the trajectory cost, Z is the partition function
that normalizes pвҲ—(u) into a valid distribution, and О» is the
temperature. Since pвҲ—(u) is generally intractable to sample
from, we introduce a variational proposal distribution Q
with density q(u). The goal of the variational inference
is to minimize the KL divergence between the proposal
distribution and the target posterior defined as:
DKL(Q вҲҘPвҲ—) = EQ

log q(u)
pвҲ—(u)

= EQ [log q(u) вҲ’log pвҲ—(u)] .
(5)
By substituting in the definition from Equation (4), we have:
DKL(Q вҲҘPвҲ—) = EQ

log q(u) + 1
О»J(u) вҲ’log p(u) + log Z

= EQ
 1
О»J(u)

+ EQ[log q(u) вҲ’log p(u)] + log Z
вҲқEQ
 1
О»J(u)

+ EQ[log q(u) вҲ’log p(u)] .
(6)
where Z is a constant as it is independent of the variational
distribution q(u).
From here, approaches such as MPPI [22] assume Gaus-
sian distributions for the prior and proposal distributions,
thereby reducing the problem to minimizing the expected
trajectory cost and the quadratic control effort over the
control sequence. However, this assumption is inadequate
when optimizing STL robustness for tasks with complex
logical structure, where the cost landscape is multi-modal.
We assume a uniform prior P : u вҲјUnif(umin, umax),
p(u) =
1
V 1[umin,umax](u). Where V is the volume of the
control space and 1 is the indicator function for inside
the control bounds. This choice reflects an unbiased prior
over admissible control sequences, so all structure in the
posterior arises solely from the trajectory cost. The new
target posterior PвҲ—is now an exponentiated cost surface
restricted by hard constraints:
pвҲ—(u) =
1
ZV exp

вҲ’1
О»J(u)

В· 1[umin,umax](u).
(7)
We obtain the free energy by multiplying Equation (6) by
the temperature О»:
F = EQ[J(u)] + О»EQ[log q(u)] вҲ’О»EQ[log p(u)].
Since our prior is now uniform, the logarithm is now
constant: EQ log p(u) = вҲ’log(V ) and irrelevant in the
minimization. Furthermore, the term вҲ’EQ [log q(u)] is the
Shannon entropy H(Q) of the proposal distribution. Hence
the new objective now becomes minimizing:
F = EQ[J(u)] вҲ’О»H(Q).
(8)
In other words, the objective becomes minimizing the ex-
pected cost and maximizing the entropy of the proposal
distribution. Note that the objectives in Equation (8) exactly
mirror the formulation used in MaxEntRL [28], which aug-
ments standard cost minimization with an entropy maximiza-
tion term. Algorithms like Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) [29] have
demonstrated practical success of the MaxEntRL principle.
However, to effectively maintain this entropy and transport
the non-parametric particle population across the complex
STL landscape, we require a specialized inference mech-
anism. In the next section, we introduce Stein Variational
Gradient Descent [24] to solve this exact problem.
B. Stein Variational Gradient Descent
SVGD [24] is a general-purpose iterative variational in-
ference algorithm, which transports a set of particles to
match the target distribution. SVGD first initializes particles
{u0
i }N
i=1, step size Пө, and kernel K. In each iteration, the
particles are updated by ut+1
i
= ut
i + ПөПҶвҲ—(ut
i). The function
ПҶвҲ—(В·) characterizes the optimal perturbation that maximally
decreases the KL-divergence between the proposal distribu-
tion and the target posterior:
ПҶвҲ—(ui) =
1
N
N
X
j=1

K(uj, ui)вҲҮuj log p(uj)
|
{z
}
Attractive Force
+ вҲҮujK(uj, ui)
|
{z
}
Repulsive Force

. (9)


--- Page 4 ---
Intuitively, the two terms in the summation represent the
attraction force towards the target distribution and the repul-
sion force generated by the kernel to prevent particles from
collapsing, respectively. Note that the kernel appears in both
terms, enabling вҖңinformation sharingвҖқ between particles to
determine the best optimization direction. Without the kernel
multiplication, the attraction force reduces to individual
gradient descent. Next, we will show how SVGD is used
to perform path-integral optimization
C. STL-guided Stein Variational Path Integral Optimization
Following SVGD, the proposal distribution Q is now
represented non-parametrically by a population of particles
{u}N
i=0. Instead of taking a softmax-weighted average of
rollouts, as in Importance Sampling, we use SVGD updates
to push particles in Q towards matching the target posterior
pвҲ—(u). SVGD relies on the derivative of the targetвҖҷs log
probability to minimize the expected cost. By taking the log
of our target posterior in Equation (7):
log pвҲ—(u) = вҲ’1
О»J(u) вҲ’log Z вҲ’log V,
вҲҮu log pвҲ—(u) = вҲҮu

вҲ’1
О»J(u) вҲ’log Z вҲ’log V

= вҲ’1
О»вҲҮuJ(u),
u вҲҲ[umin, umax].
(10)
With a uniform prior, the score function of the target
posterior depends solely on the gradient of the trajectory
cost, simplifying the SVGD update and avoiding additional
prior-induced forces. For the derivation for transforming the
entropy of the proposal in Equation (8) into the kernel
repulsion force, we refer the readers to [24]. The general
idea is to increase the volume of the proposal distribution
by maximizing the divergence of a vector field (the trace of
the Jacobian of the proposal distribution). When restricting
that vector field to a reproducing kernel Hilbert space, the
divergence of the vector field is exactly the gradient of
the chosen kernel. In summary, the optimal perturbation
direction ПҶвҲ—(u) for a particle ui is given by the following
SVGD update:
ПҶвҲ—(ui) =
1
N
N
X
j=1
"
вҲ’1
О»K(uj, ui)вҲҮujJ(uj) + вҲҮujK(uj, ui)
#
,
ui вҶҗui + ПөПҶвҲ—(ui),
(11)
where K(В·, В·) is a positive definite kernel.
We choose negative STL robustness evaluated on the state
trajectory xi,0:H induced by a particle ui as the cost function:
J(ui) = вҲ’ПҒП•(xi,0:H).
(12)
Following [25], we use a smooth RBF kernel:
KRBF(ui, uj) = exp
вҲ’вҲҘui вҲ’uj вҲҘ2
2
h

,
(13)
where h is the median heuristic on the population of all
particles: h = median({ui})2/ log(N вҲ’1). For a specific
STL formula П•, the SVGD update step in our algorithm is
as follows:
ПҶвҲ—(ui) = 1
N
N
X
j=1
 1
О»KRBF(uj, ui)вҲҮujПҒП• (xj,0:H)
+ вҲҮujKRBF(uj, ui)

,
ui вҶҗui + ПөПҶвҲ—(ui).
(14)
Note that we assume that the dynamics and integration
method chosen to generate the state trajectory are differ-
entiable. Recent advances in computation frameworks such
as JAX [30] make automatic differentiation through physics
simulations tractable.
D. Algorithm Summary
Algorithm 1 STL-SVPIO
Require: Initial state x0, simulation function sim(), STL
specification П•, horizon H, number of particles N,
optimization iterations M, step size Пө, temperature О».
1: Initialize: Sample {u}N
i=1 вҲјUnif(umin, umax)
2: for iter m вҶҗ1 to M do
3:
вҲҖij Compute K(ui, uj), вҲҮuiK(uj, ui)
4:
for i вҶҗ1 to N do
в–·In parallel
5:
Rollout all particles xi
0:H вҶҗsim(xi
0, ui)
6:
Compute attraction si = вҲ’1
О»вҲҮuiПҒП•(xi
0:H)
7:
вҲҶui вҶҗ1
N
PN
j=1

K(uj, ui)sj + вҲҮujK(uj, ui)

8:
ui вҶҗui + ПөвҲҶui
9:
end for
10: end for
return arg maxuвҲҲ{uN
i=1} ПҒП•
i
The STL-SVPIO algorithm (Algorithm 1) proceeds as
follows: First, sample N particles as control sequences that
spans the entire horizon H from a uniform distribution
ui
0:H вҲјUnif(umin, umax). In each iteration, for each par-
ticle in parallel, rollout the dynamics f to get trajectories
xi
0:H with the sim() function. Next, compute the pairwise
kernel value and gradient for each particle. Then compute
STL robustness ПҒП• and its gradient вҲҮuПҒП• as the attraction
force. Lastly, transport all particles using SVGD. At the end
of the iterations, we select the particle with the highest STL
robustness as the final solution.
V. EXPERIMENTS
In this section, we evaluate STL-SVPIO through three
sets of experiments. First, we consider a simple reach-avoid
task with 2D point-mass dynamics to demonstrate the wide
range of baselines considered. Second, we demonstrate the
effectiveness and efficiency of STL-SVPIO on more complex
scenarios in both single- and multi-agent settings. For the
single-agent case, we focus on long-horizon planning in
cluttered environments. For the multi-agent case, we evaluate
the methodsвҖҷ ability to enforce task-completion ordering,


--- Page 5 ---
achieve synchronized goal satisfaction, and resolve inter-
agent conflicts. Finally, we show that STL-SVPIO gener-
alizes to highly agile motion planning tasks with nonlinear
dynamics, without requiring any modification to the algo-
rithm.
We now introduce the baselines used for comparison.
These baselines are chosen to highlight different aspects of
our proposed method.
вҖў MPPI+Heuristics
[22] is a sampling-based baseline
with a handcrafted stage and terminal costs based on
Euclidean distances to obstacles and the goal.
вҖў SVMPC [25] is an ablation for STL-SVPIO, isolating
the effect of exact STL gradients versus finite-difference
approximations.
вҖў DPI [23] is a path integral baseline that was designed
to solve STL specifications.
вҖў STLCG++ [15], [16] is another ablation, isolating the
effect of STL-SVPIOвҖҷs particle-based SVGD method
versus direct gradient descent.
вҖў MILP [8], [31] is an STL solver with linear encoding.
In all experiments, each task is defined with an STL
specification, and the robustness value of a trajectory is
calculated following the recursive definition in Section III-A.
To evaluate the methods, the main metric we focus on is the
robustness value each methodвҖҷs final solution achieves. The
higher the robustness, the better. A task is satisfied when the
robustness value is greater than zero. We use the stljax
library from STLCG++ [16] for all robustness evaluation.
For sampling-based methods, we evaluate performance over
multiple different random seeds in Section V-B. All experi-
ments are conducted on NVDIA RTX A6000 GPUs.
A. Single Agent Reach Avoid
We first evaluate STL-SVPIO on a simple single-agent
reachвҖ“avoid task using a 2D point mass model. The system is
a double integrator with acceleration inputs. The task requires
Fig. 2.
Selected trajectories of each method through iterations.
the agent to always avoid a circular obstacle, and eventually
reach the square goal zone within the planning horizon H.
The corresponding STL specification is:
П•ra := в–Ў
[0,H] Вөavoid(xt) вҲ§вҷў
[0,H]
Вөin box(xt),
(15)
with predicates:
Вөavoid(xt) := вҲҘxt вҲ’cobs вҲҘ2> robs,
Вөin box(xt) := вҲҘxt вҲ’cgoal вҲҘвҲһвүӨ1
2hgoal,
where cobs and robs denote the obstacle center and radius,
while cgoal and hgoal define the center and the side length of
the goal zone.
Method
# Samples # Iterations
Runtime (ms) Robustness
STL-SVPIO
10
20
19.152
0.108
MPPI
10
200
187.125
0.005
SVMPC
64
200
178.389
-0.098
DPI
1024
200
194.492
0.179
STLCG++
N/A
200
926.704
-0.068
MILP
N/A
N/A
324.578
0.495
TABLE I
REACH AVOID TASK PARAMETERS AND ROBUSTNESS
Figure 2 shows each algorithmвҖҷs selected trajectories dur-
ing optimization, and Table I compares parameters used, run-
time, and final robustness values produced by each method
on the single-agent reachвҖ“avoid task. SVMPC and STLCG++
only find solutions that violate the specification by not arriv-
ing at the goal zone at the final step. All other methods find
satisfying solutions. Our method achieves positive robustness
with the lowest number of samples and iterations. While
MPPI+Heuristics performs well with handcrafted costs, it
does not naturally generalize beyond this task. In this simple
setting, the finite-difference gradients used by SVMPC are
sufficient to reach near-feasible solutions but remain less
efficient than those of STL-SVPIO. DPI already exhibits
high sample usage in a simple task. STLCG++ shows slow
convergence. MILP achieves the best satisfaction margin
while maintaining a reasonable solve time. However, we
show in subsequent experiments that it does not scale with
more complicated STL formulae. Although most methods
succeed on this reachвҖ“avoid task, the efficiency gap becomes
more pronounced in the more complex single- and multi-
agent experiments.
B. More Complex 2D Point Mass Tasks
We next evaluate STL-SVPIO on a set of more chal-
lenging single- and multi-agent tasks. All agents are the
same 2D point-mass as in Section V-A. Before introducing
each task, we define a set of atomic predicates to construct
the corresponding STL formulae. Agents are indexed by
i вҲҲ{0, . . . , n}, obstacles by o, and zones by z. Agent
collision radii are denoted by rcol. Obstacle centers and radii
are denoted by c and r. We define the following predicates:
вҖў Obstacle avoidance for agent i and obstacle o at time t:
Вөavoid(i, o, t) := вҲҘxi(t) вҲ’cobs
o вҲҘ2 > robs
o ,
вҖў Agent i inside circular zone z at time t:
Вөin(i, z, t) := вҲҘxi(t) вҲ’czone
z
вҲҘ2 вүӨrzone
z
,
вҖў Agent i inside box zone z with side lengths hx
z and hy
z
with center (czone
x
, czone
y
):
Вөin box(i, z, t) := min{hx
z вҲ’|(xi(t))x вҲ’czone
x
|,
hy
z вҲ’|(xi(t))y вҲ’czone
y
|} > 0,


--- Page 6 ---
Fig. 3.
Aggregated average robustness and satisfaction rate of methods. All algorithms besides MILP were tested across 100 different random sampling
seeds. Each solution with positive robustness counts as a satisfaction. Multiagent Button corresponds to the scenario in Section V-B.2, Multiagent Corridor
corresponds to the scenario in Section V-B.4, Multiagent Sync Goals corresponds to the scenario in Section V-B.3, and Long Horizon corresponds to the
scenario in Section V-B.1. Gurobi was given a 10-hour time limit to solve the MILP.
вҖў Pairwise collision avoidance between agents i and j:
Вөcol(i, j, t) := вҲҘxi(t) вҲ’xj(t)вҲҘ2 вүҘ2rcol.
We design four experiments:
Fig. 4.
Long horizon trajectory synthesized by STL-SVPIO through a
cluttered obstacle field. Dark green and red markers indicate the start and
final states, respectively.
1) Long Horizon Planning in Cluttered Environments: In
this task (Figure 4), a single agent must traverse a cluttered
environment and reach all assigned goals within a 600-step
horizon. The STL specification is:
П•lh :=
 ^
o
в–Ў
[0,H] Вөavoid(1, o, t)

вҲ§
 ^
z
вҷў
[0,H]
Вөin(1, z, t)

This scenario evaluates the ability to find feasible trajectories
through narrow passages over long horizons.
2) Enforcing Task Completion Order: In this multi-agent
task (Figure 5), agent 1 must reach goal A but may only do
so after agent 2 activates button B. Agent 2 must eventually
reach goal C. Both agents must avoid all obstacles at all
times. Gated entry is specified by:
П•gated entry := В¬Вөin(2, C, t)UВөin(1, B, t).
The full STL specification for this task is:
П•co :=
 ^
o
в–Ў
[0,H] Вөavoid(1, o, t)

вҲ§
 ^
o
в–Ў
[0,H] Вөavoid(2, o, t)

вҲ§

вҷў
[0,H]
Вөin(1, A, t)

вҲ§

вҷў
[0,H]
Вөin(2, C, t)

вҲ§П•gated entry.
This task tests strict temporal ordering constraints in a
coupled multi-agent setting.
Fig. 5.
STL-SVPIO synthesizes a multi-agent trajectory that satisfies strict
ordering constraints between task events.
3) Synchronized Task Completion: In this task (Figure 6),
all agents must reach their respective goals within a shared
but unspecified time window of width 2Оҙ, while avoiding
collisions. The STL specification of this task is:
П•sg :=
 ^
i<j
в–Ў
[0,H] Вөcol(i, j, t)

вҲ§
вҷў
[0,HвҲ’2Оҙ]
 ^
i
вҷў
[0,2Оҙ]
Вөin(i, i, t)

.
This scenario evaluates coordination in high-dimensional
joint spaces under tight temporal coupling.
Fig. 6.
A solution generated by STL-SVPIO in which all agents reach
their assigned goals within a shared, unspecified time window.
4) Conflict Resolution: In this task (Figure 7), agents must
traverse a wall at x = 0 through a narrow corridor, allowing
only one agent inside the corridor at any time. Let
П•x+(i, t) := (xi(t))x вүҘ0,
and define exclusive corridor occupancy:
П•occ(i, j, z, t) := В¬
␀Вөin box(i, z, t) вҲ§Вөin box(j, z, t)

.


--- Page 7 ---
We index the rectangular boxes that define the walls with w,
and the corridor rectangular box as zcor. The STL specifica-
tion used for this task is:
П•cr =
 ^
i<j
в–Ў
[0,H] Вөcol(i, j, t)

вҲ§
 ^
i
^
w
в–Ў
[0,H] В¬Вөin box(i, w, t)

вҲ§
 ^
i
вҷў
[0,H]
Вөin box(i, zcor, t)

вҲ§
 ^
i
вҷў
[0,H]
Вөx+(i, t)

вҲ§
 ^
i<j
в–Ў
[0,H] П•occ(i, j, zcor, t)

This task enforces both collision avoidance and implicit
queuing behavior in a tightly constrained joint space.
Fig. 7.
STL-SVPIO plans collision-free trajectories that enforce sequential
corridor occupancy among multiple agents.
Baseline Comparison Results: We compare STL-SVPIO
against DPI, SVMPC, STLCG++, and MILP using average
robustness, satisfaction rate, and runtime (Figure 3). MPPI
with handcrafted costs is omitted, as designing dense stage
and terminal costs for these intertwined temporal constraints
is impractical. All stochastic methods are evaluated over 100
random seeds, while MILP is solved once per task. For each
algorithm and scenario, we perform an extensive hyperpa-
rameter sweep over 100 to 400 configurations, depending on
the method, and report the best-performing results to ensure
a fair and best-effort comparison.
MILP demonstrates exactness when solutions are found,
but suffers from severe scalability limitations. With a 10-
hour time limit, MILP succeeds only in the Button (V-B.2)
and Sync Goals (V-B.3) tasks and exhibits runtimes orders of
magnitude longer. In the Corridor task, linear approximations
of Euclidean distance predicates lead to solutions that satisfy
the MILP but violate the original STL specification, resulting
in zero satisfaction. In the Long-Horizon task, MILP times
out due to problem size and geometric complexity.
Compared to STLCG++, STL-SVPIO benefits from main-
taining a particle population. Although both methods use
exact STL gradients, STLCG++ shows high variance and
fails in the Corridor (V-B.4) and Long-Horizon (V-B.1) tasks
due to high sensitivity to initialization and local minima.
Among sampling-based methods, STL-SVPIO is the only
approach that succeeds across all scenarios. DPI struggles
due to its uni-modal proposal distribution and reliance on
terminal costs, making successful samples extremely rare in
highly non-convex STL landscapes, even with 1024вҖ“2048
samples per iteration, compared to 10 particles for STL-
SVPIO. SVMPC performs well in simpler tasks with 64
particles, but finite-difference gradients become unreliable
in harder scenarios and incur high effective sampling costs
(8x number of particles used) when estimating the gradient.
Overall, these results demonstrate that combining exact STL
gradients with particle-based variational updates enables ro-
bust and scalable planning in complex multi-agent settings.
C. Nonlinear Dynamic Motion Planning
We next demonstrate that STL-SVPIO generalizes beyond
2D point-mass systems to motion planning problems with
highly nonlinear dynamics. We use MuJoCo MJX [32], a
JAX [30] based physics engine that supports GPU parallel
simulation and automatic differentiation under appropriate
solver and integrator settings. In our implementation, MJX
is used as the parallel forward dynamics module (Algorithm
1, line 7), enabling end-to-end differentiability through the
simulation.
Fig. 8.
Rollout snapshots of a torque-controlled Franka Panda trajectory
synthesized by STL-SVPIO. The end effector first reaches the yellow goal
and subsequently reaches the green goal.
1) Franka Panda Goal Reach: Figure 8 shows a 7-DoF
Franka Panda manipulation task in which the end effector
must reach multiple goal regions. Let xee(t) denote the end-
effector position, and let each goal g be defined by center cg
radius rg. The reach predicate is:
Вөreach(g, t) := вҲҘxee(t) вҲ’cgвҲҘ2 вүӨrg,
and the STL specification is:
П•panda =
^
g
вҷў
[0,H]
Вөreach(g, t).
STL-SVPIO requires no algorithmic modification beyond
hyperparameter tuning. Control particles are defined directly
in the joint torque space, and no inverse kinematics are
used. Using only 10 particles and 300 iterations, STL-SVPIO
successfully synthesizes a torque-level control sequence that
reaches all goals in 634.85 seconds, with a final robustness
value of 0.029, satisfying the STL specification. The solu-
tionвҖҷs trajectory are shown in Figure 8 as snapshots.
Fig. 9. Rollout snapshots of a Half-Cheetah back flip trajectory synthesized
by STL-SVPIO.
2) Half Cheetah Back Flip: Highly agile motions such as
back flips are difficult to specify and optimize using standard
reward functions. In reinforcement learning, such behaviors
often rely on extensive reward shaping, human feedback [33],
or open-ended skill discovery [34]. In contrast, we specify
the desired physical effects of a back flip directly using STL.
Let z(t) denote the torso height, Оё(t) the torso pitch angle,
Пү(t) the pitch rate. We define predicates Вөz(t) := z(t) >
zmin for torso height, ВөПү(t) := |Пү(t)| < Пүmax for safe pitch


--- Page 8 ---
rate, and Вөflip(t) := вҲ’(Оё(t) вҲ’Оё(0)) > Оёtarget вҲ’ПөОё for flip
completion. The full STL specification is:
П•back flip = в–Ў
[0,H] Вөz(t) вҲ§в–Ў
[0,H] ВөПү(t) вҲ§
вҷў
[HвҲ’Оҙ,H]
Вөflip(t).
Figure 9 shows the resulting rollout. STL-VPIO obtained
a solution using 10 particles and 300 iterations in 593.22
seconds, with a final robustness value of 0.168, satisfying
the specification. Despite the highly nonlinear dynamics
and discontinuous contact events, STL-SVPIO successfully
discovers an agile backflip motion without any task-specific
modifications to the algorithm.
VI. CONCLUSION AND LIMITATION
We presented STL-SVPIO, a variational framework that
synthesizes continuous control trajectories from STL speci-
fications. By integrating differentiable STL robustness with
SVGD and differentiable physics, STL-SVPIO efficiently
navigates long-horizon, non-convex landscapes. It outper-
forms prior baselines in robustness and scalability, gener-
alizing seamlessly to complex nonlinear systems.
However, our reliance on auto-differentiable physics
means high-fidelity simulations incur steep memory and run-
time costs. Additionally, contact dynamics introduce discon-
tinuities that require careful hyperparameter tuning. Future
work will extend STL-SVPIO to real-world tasks and mo-
tion planning for physical platforms, including quadrupeds,
humanoids, and quadrotors.
ACKNOWLEDGMENT
Gemini and ChatGPT were used for fixing grammatical
errors and improving clarity on the non-technical sections of
the manuscript. Codex was used to assist in implementing
experimental code. All technical content, experimental de-
sign, results, and conclusions were developed, verified, and
carefully vetted by the authors.
